parsed.title: SuCo: Sufficiency-guided Continuous Adaptive Reasoning
parsed.field: Efficient reasoning / Large Reasoning Models (LLM reasoning)
parsed.abstract: The paper introduces Minimal Sufficient CoT (MSC), defined as the shortest prefix of a CoT trajectory adequate to produce the correct answer. The authors empirically show MSC reduces tokens while improving accuracy. They propose SuCo, a two-stage training framework: Stage I (MSC-Aligned Fine-Tuning, MFT) constructs MSC data with problem-adaptive sufficiency thresholds and fine-tunes the model; Stage II (Sufficiency-Aware Policy Optimization, SAPO) uses RL with a dynamic complexity pool and sufficiency-aware rewards that penalize over- and under-thinking. Experiments on math, code, and science benchmarks show improvements in both accuracy and reasoning efficiency.

sections.introduction: LRMs like DeepSeek-R1 and o1 achieve strong reasoning via CoT but suffer from redundant reasoning, generating long chains even for simple queries. Existing Adaptive LRMs (ALRMs) use discrete modes (user-controlled switches or model-driven binary/multi-mode selection) and lack a principled criterion for sufficiency. The authors pose: can models do better with less reasoning? They answer yes by introducing MSC, the shortest reasoning prefix that suffices for correct answers, and show on MATH that MSC outperforms full CoT across all difficulty levels with far fewer tokens. They propose SuCo for continuous reasoning control via MFT + SAPO.
sections.related_work: Reviews LRMs (DeepSeek-R1, o1, Qwen3) and ALRMs grouped into binary triggering (AdaCoT, AdaptThink, LHRMs), multi-mode reasoning (SABER, ThinkDial), and fine-grained control (ThinkPrune, CyclicReflex, AlphaOne). Notes existing ALRMs rely on discrete modes and coarse supervision. SuCo differs by introducing a continuous sufficiency-grounded criterion (MSC).
sections.method: Defines reasoning sufficiency as the geometric mean of per-token probabilities of the ground-truth answer given (x, z). A trajectory is delta-sufficient if sufficiency >= delta. MSC is the shortest sentence-level prefix that is delta-sufficient. Uses a problem-adaptive threshold delta(x) = delta_0 + alpha * C(x) where C(x) is a percentile-based complexity proxy (rank of reasoning length). Stage I (MFT): generates full CoT with an LRM, computes per-sample thresholds and finds shortest sufficient prefix, refines via a refinement model for coherence, then SFT on (x, MSC, answer). Empty MSCs allowed if prefix too short. Stage II (SAPO): GRPO-based RL. Maintains an EMA-updated dynamic complexity pool tracking trajectory lengths to recompute adaptive thresholds online. Reward = correctness + format + beta * sufficiency reward, where sufficiency reward penalizes over-thinking (length > t* + epsilon) and under-thinking (length < t* and answer wrong).
sections.experiments: Trained on Qwen2.5-Math-Base 1.5B and 7B. Training data: 270,011 samples curated from Llama-Nemotron, Mixture-of-Thoughts, OpenR1-Math-220k, OpenCodeReasoning, s1K-1.1. Refinement and quality assessment with Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B-Instruct. Evaluated on GSM8K, MATH-500, AMC23, AIME25, MBPP, LiveCodeBench-V6, MMLU-STEM, GPQA-Diamond. Baselines: Qwen2.5-Math-Base/Instruct, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen, AdaCoT, AdaptThink, S-GRPO, LHRMs. Ablations on threshold, complexity estimation, refinement, DCP, sufficiency reward, L_min, EMA rate, tolerance epsilon, sufficiency metric, intra/cross-domain percentile. Additional analysis on difficulty-conditioned length, OOD generalization (StrategyQA, CSQA, AlpacaEval), cross-model robustness, and empty CoT rates.
sections.results: SuCo achieves average accuracy 53.1% (1.5B) and 72.1% (7B), beating DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen by ~17.5%/14.1% relative and LHRMs by ~5.1%. Reduces tokens by 74.1% (1.5B) and 75.8% (7B) vs. R1-Distill. On AIME25 7B: +24.1% accuracy with 75.3% fewer tokens. Ablations show MFT alone beats full CoT SFT (45.7% vs 38.4%). SAPO further refines allocation: shrinks length on easy benchmarks, expands on hard ones. OOD: SuCo outperforms baselines on StrategyQA, CSQA, AlpacaEval. Calibrator-agnostic: MSC supervision transfers across model families.
sections.limitations: MSC construction requires ground-truth answers (limits open-ended tasks). MFT depends on data distilled from strong LRMs and uses an 80B refinement model.
sections.conclusion: SuCo, based on MSC, enables continuous adaptive reasoning calibrated to problem difficulty without discrete modes or external controllers. Achieves higher accuracy with fewer tokens across math, code, and science. Future work: open-ended sufficiency estimation and agentic settings.

main_claims: Minimal Sufficient CoT (MSC) — the shortest sufficient reasoning prefix — yields higher accuracy than full CoT across difficulty levels.
A geometric-mean sufficiency score with a problem-adaptive threshold provides a principled criterion for when reasoning is sufficient.
Two-stage training (MFT + SAPO) enables continuous, autonomous reasoning effort allocation without discrete modes.
Dynamic complexity pool with EMA updates and sufficiency-aware rewards (penalizing over- and under-thinking) stabilize and improve RL training.
SuCo outperforms full-CoT baselines (R1-Distill) and adaptive baselines (AdaCoT, AdaptThink, S-GRPO, LHRMs) in both accuracy and token efficiency.
Adaptive reasoning learned by SuCo generalizes to OOD tasks (StrategyQA, CSQA, AlpacaEval) and is robust across calibrator models.
key_contributions: Formalization of Minimal Sufficient CoT (MSC) with a geometric-mean sufficiency criterion and problem-adaptive threshold.
SuCo two-stage training framework (MFT + SAPO) for continuous adaptive reasoning.
Dynamic complexity pool with EMA and sufficiency-aware reward shaping for online RL.
Comprehensive empirical validation across math, code, science, OOD tasks, and calibrator models.
experimental_results: On MATH difficulty levels, MSC achieves higher accuracy with substantially fewer tokens than full CoT (Figure 1).
SuCo 1.5B reaches 53.1% avg accuracy vs LHRMs 50.5% and R1-Distill 45.2%; SuCo 7B reaches 72.1% vs LHRMs 68.6% and R1-Distill 63.2%.
SuCo reduces avg response tokens by ~74-76% vs R1-Distill; on AIME25 7B: 61.7% accuracy with 2,679 tokens vs R1-Distill 49.7% with 10,833.
MFT alone (45.7%) outperforms full CoT SFT (38.4%) with only ~26% of the tokens.
Ablation: removing DCP (52.9%) or sufficiency reward (52.7%) degrades SAPO performance (53.1%).
Geometric mean sufficiency metric outperforms joint probability (40.1%) and arithmetic mean (43.1%); ours: 45.7%.
OOD: SuCo beats R1-Distill on StrategyQA (55.7 vs 53.3), CSQA (49.3 vs 45.0), AlpacaEval (LC-WR 2.4 vs 1.05), with fewer or comparable tokens.
Cross-calibrator robustness: MSC data from Qwen3-4B, Qwen3-14B, and DS-R1-Distill-7B all outperform full CoT SFT on Qwen2.5-1.5B and Llama-3.2-3B targets.
Empty CoT rate decreases with problem difficulty (L1>L5) and is higher for 7B than 1.5B (Figure 7).
assumptions_and_limitations: MSC requires ground-truth answers to compute sufficiency, limiting application to open-ended tasks.
Reasoning length is used as a proxy for problem complexity; assumed monotonic with difficulty.
Sufficiency is computed under the policy's own probability, which may shift during training (addressed partially via DCP).
MFT relies on distillation data from strong LRMs and uses Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B-Instruct for MSC refinement and quality filtering.
Sentence-level segmentation assumed to align with atomic reasoning steps.
Experiments limited to Qwen2.5-Math-Base 1.5B/7B; no scaling beyond 7B or other foundation families in main results.

equations_algorithms: Eq. 1: Sufficiency S_theta(z|x,y*) = (prod_i pi_theta(y*_i|x,z,y*_<i))^(1/|y*|), geometric mean of token probabilities.
Eq. 2: delta-MSC definition requiring sufficiency at t* and minimality for all t < t*.
Eq. 3: Problem-adaptive threshold delta(x) = delta_0 + alpha * C(x).
Eq. 8: SFT loss over MSC dataset (NLL on MSC and answer tokens).
Eq. 9: EMA update of dynamic complexity pool ||z^avg_i||.
Eq. 10-11: Total reward = correctness + format + beta * sufficiency, with sufficiency penalizing over-thinking (Lz > t*+epsilon) and under-thinking (Lz < t* and y != y*).
Algorithm 1: MSC dataset construction (full CoT generation, threshold computation, prefix scan, refinement).

parsed.field_and_subfield: Efficient reasoning for Large Reasoning Models (LRMs); adaptive Chain-of-Thought control in LLMs
parsed.problem_addressed: Large Reasoning Models generate excessively long Chain-of-Thought trajectories even for simple queries, inflating compute and latency. Existing Adaptive LRMs rely on discrete reasoning modes or fixed budget tiers without a principled criterion for when reasoning is sufficient.
parsed.research_question: Can a principled, continuous criterion of reasoning sufficiency be defined and operationalized so that models autonomously calibrate reasoning effort to problem difficulty, achieving higher accuracy with fewer tokens than full CoT and discrete-mode adaptive baselines?
parsed.one_paragraph_summary: The paper introduces Minimal Sufficient CoT (MSC), defined as the shortest sentence-level prefix of a CoT trajectory whose geometric-mean per-token probability of the ground-truth answer exceeds a problem-adaptive threshold δ(x) = δ0 + α·C(x), where C(x) is a percentile rank of reasoning length used as a complexity proxy. Building on MSC, the authors propose SuCo, a two-stage training framework: (i) MSC-Aligned Fine-Tuning (MFT) constructs an MSC dataset from full CoT trajectories (with LLM-based refinement) and performs SFT; (ii) Sufficiency-Aware Policy Optimization (SAPO) extends GRPO with a dynamic complexity pool (EMA-updated trajectory lengths) and a sufficiency-aware reward that penalizes both over- and under-thinking. Experiments on Qwen2.5-Math-Base 1.5B/7B across 8 math/code/science benchmarks show SuCo outperforms DeepSeek-R1-Distill and adaptive baselines (AdaCoT, AdaptThink, S-GRPO, LHRMs) in both accuracy and token efficiency, with additional OOD and cross-calibrator robustness results.
parsed.problem_statement: Given a question x with ground-truth answer y*, find the shortest reasoning prefix z_<t* such that the policy assigns sufficient probability mass to y* conditioned on (x, z_<t*), and train a model to autonomously produce such minimal sufficient reasoning along a continuous (rather than discrete-mode) spectrum.
main_contributions: Formalization of Minimal Sufficient CoT (MSC) using a geometric-mean per-token sufficiency score and a problem-adaptive threshold tied to a percentile-based complexity proxy.

A two-stage training framework (SuCo) combining MSC-aligned SFT (MFT) and Sufficiency-Aware Policy Optimization (SAPO), the latter introducing a dynamic complexity pool via EMA and a reward shaping scheme penalizing both over- and under-thinking.

Empirical demonstration across math, code, and science benchmarks (at 1.5B/7B scales) that SuCo improves accuracy while substantially reducing reasoning tokens compared to full-CoT and discrete-mode adaptive baselines.
Auxiliary analyses showing OOD generalization (StrategyQA, CSQA, AlpacaEval), cross-calibrator robustness of constructed MSC data, and difficulty-conditioned length allocation.

claim_evidence_map.claim: MSC achieves higher accuracy than full CoT with far fewer tokens across difficulty levels.
claim_evidence_map.evidence_in_paper: Figure 1 (Qwen3-8B on MATH L1–L5) shows MSC bars/lines have lower token counts and higher accuracy than full CoT at every difficulty level.

claim_evidence_map.claim: The geometric-mean sufficiency formulation is superior to joint probability and arithmetic mean.
claim_evidence_map.evidence_in_paper: Appendix Table 8: geometric mean reaches 45.7% accuracy at 1,344 inference tokens vs 40.1%/2,573 (joint) and 43.1%/1,578 (arithmetic).
Table 2 (Threshold section): adaptive MFT achieves 45.7% avg accuracy at 1,344 tokens, surpassing all static δ ∈ {0.5,...,0.9} configurations (best static δ=0.7 at 44.0%).

claim_evidence_map.claim: SuCo outperforms full-CoT and adaptive baselines on accuracy and tokens.
claim_evidence_map.evidence_in_paper: Table 1: SuCo-1.5B avg 53.1% acc / 1,483 tokens vs LHRMs 50.5/2,055 and R1-Distill 45.2/5,736; SuCo-7B avg 72.1/1,267 vs LHRMs 68.6/1,891 and R1-Distill 63.2/5,239.

claim_evidence_map.claim: Dynamic Complexity Pool and Sufficiency-Aware Reward each contribute to SAPO performance.
claim_evidence_map.evidence_in_paper: Table 3: w/o DCP drops to 52.9% / 1,642 tokens, w/o R_suff to 52.7%/2,053 tokens, vs full SAPO 53.1%/1,483.

claim_evidence_map.claim: SAPO allocates more tokens on hard benchmarks and fewer on easy ones (adaptive allocation rather than uniform compression).
claim_evidence_map.evidence_in_paper: Figure 5: MFT→SAPO reduces tokens on GSM8K (382→304) and MATH500 (884→538) while increasing on AMC23, AIME25, Live-V6 with accuracy gains.
Table 4: SuCo achieves 55.7/49.3/2.4 on StrategyQA/CSQA/AlpacaEval, outperforming R1-Distill (53.3/45.0/1.05) with fewer or comparable tokens.

claim_evidence_map.claim: MSC supervision is robust across calibrator models.
claim_evidence_map.evidence_in_paper: Table 5: MSC data from Qwen3-4B, Qwen3-14B, and DS-R1-Distill-7B all outperform Full CoT SFT on Qwen2.5-1.5B and Llama-3.2-3B target models (44.x vs 37–38%).

claim_evidence_map.claim: SuCo shows stronger difficulty-conditioned length scaling than the base LRM.
claim_evidence_map.evidence_in_paper: Figure 6 caption/text: L5/L1 mean token ratio ≈ 5.5× for SuCo vs ≈ 3.1× for DeepSeek-R1-Distill-1.5B; Level 1 uses 89% fewer tokens than base LRM.

claim_evidence_map.claim: MFT alone surpasses Full CoT SFT.
claim_evidence_map.evidence_in_paper: Table 2 Overall: MFT 45.7% / 1,344 tokens vs Full 38.4% / 5,082 tokens (only ~26% of full CoT tokens).

sections.name: Abstract
sections.summary: Introduces MSC as the shortest CoT prefix sufficient to produce the correct answer and SuCo, a two-stage framework (MFT + SAPO) for continuous adaptive reasoning. Claims accuracy and efficiency gains across math, code, and science.
key_claims: MSC reduces tokens and improves accuracy across difficulty levels.
SuCo enables continuous reasoning control without discrete modes.
SAPO's sufficiency-aware rewards penalize both over- and under-thinking.
supporting_evidence: Forward references to Figure 1 (MATH difficulty levels) and main experiments.
issues: Abstract states 'better with less reasoning' without specifying that MSC is computed using ground-truth answers, which may mislead readers about the practical accuracy advantage.
missing_information: No quantitative summary of gains in abstract.
No mention that MSC requires ground-truth answers for construction (a key limitation).
ambiguous_or_unsupported: Claim that MSC 'improves accuracy across difficulty levels' is shown only on MATH with one model (Qwen3-8B in Fig.1); the abstract overgeneralizes.

sections.name: Introduction
sections.summary: Motivates MSC as a principled sufficiency criterion countering test-time scaling intuition. Argues prior ALRMs rely on discrete modes and lack a sufficiency notion. Previews SuCo and contributions.
key_claims: Existing ALRMs use discrete mode selection only.
Models can achieve better performance with less reasoning.
SuCo provides continuous, autonomous reasoning control.
supporting_evidence: Figure 1 showing MSC outperforms full CoT on MATH L1-L5 with Qwen3-8B.
issues: Figure 1 is computed with knowledge of the ground-truth answer (since MSC requires y*); this makes the 'MSC > Full CoT' comparison an oracle setup, but this caveat is not explicit in the intro.
The dichotomy 'discrete vs continuous' is somewhat overstated: ThinkPrune, AlphaOne, S-GRPO arguably operate on continuous lengths too.

The framing 'Can models actually perform better with less reasoning?' conflates two effects (truncation revealing high-confidence intermediate answers vs. actually improving reasoning), which is not disentangled.
missing_information: No discussion that MSC accuracy in Figure 1 is computed by conditioning on the optimal prefix selected using y*; whether this generalizes when y* is unknown is not addressed until training.
ambiguous_or_unsupported: Claim that prior ALRMs 'lack a principled criterion of when reasoning is sufficient' — partly true, but constrained-optimization formulations (e.g., AdaptThink) do provide some criterion.

sections.name: Related Work
sections.summary: Surveys LRMs and ALRMs, categorizing prior efficient-reasoning methods into binary triggering, multi-mode, and fine-grained control, all sharing the limitation of discrete supervision.
key_claims: Existing ALRMs regulate reasoning through discrete specified modes.
SuCo introduces continuous adaptive reasoning grounded in MSC.
supporting_evidence: Citations to AdaCoT, AdaptThink, LHRMs, SABER, ThinkDial, ThinkPrune, CyclicReflex, AlphaOne.
issues: ThinkPrune and S-GRPO are length-based RL methods that also produce continuous-length outputs; the contrast with SuCo as the unique 'continuous' approach is overstated.
Does not discuss closely related work on early-exit and confidence-based truncation in LLM inference.
missing_information: No comparison to recent work on calibration/confidence-based stopping criteria for CoT.
No mention of speculative thinking, self-consistency, or confidence-based termination methods that share a similar sufficiency intuition.
ambiguous_or_unsupported: Characterization that S-GRPO uses 'decaying rewards' is correct but underexplains the similarity to SAPO's sufficiency reward.

sections.name: Method
sections.summary: Defines reasoning sufficiency as geometric mean of per-token probabilities of y*, defines delta-MSC, problem-adaptive threshold delta(x)=delta_0 + alpha*C(x), percentile-based complexity, and the two stages MFT (SFT on MSC data) and SAPO (GRPO with dynamic complexity pool and sufficiency-aware reward).
key_claims: Geometric mean normalizes joint probability and is robust across answer lengths.
Adaptive threshold scales sufficiency demand with problem difficulty.
Dynamic complexity pool (EMA-updated) tracks policy evolution for stable RL signals.
Sufficiency reward penalizes over- and under-thinking.
supporting_evidence: Appendix A.4 ablates the geometric mean choice.
issues: Sufficiency S_theta(z|x,y*) depends on theta, but it is unclear which model computes sufficiency during MSC construction — the source LRM M_LRM, the base model πθ, or the refinement model? The text uses πθ in Eq.1 but Algorithm 1 references πθ without specifying which checkpoint. This is a non-trivial ambiguity since sufficiency is policy-dependent.

The problem-adaptive threshold ties complexity to reasoning length, but Figure 1 only shows reasoning length correlates with MATH difficulty on Qwen3-8B; the assumption may not hold uniformly (e.g., code).

Minimality is defined as 'no shorter prefix is sufficient', but if no prefix reaches delta(x), Eq.6 selects the most sufficient prefix — this breaks the formal definition of delta-MSC, yet the resulting trajectory is still labeled MSC in the dataset.
Sentence-level boundary detection is stated as 'natural atomic reasoning steps' but no segmentation procedure is specified (e.g., punctuation, newlines, special tokens).

SAPO reward Eq.11 uses 1[Lz > t*+epsilon] but t* in inference is unknown without y*; the text says t* is identified via Eq.5 using y*, meaning the reward requires ground-truth answer access during RL — consistent with verifiable RL but should be explicit.

When t* = infinity (no sufficient prefix), the over-thinking indicator 1[Lz > t*+epsilon] is always 0 and the under-thinking penalty triggers only if y is wrong; the resulting incentive structure for hard problems where no prefix is sufficient is unclear.
The refinement step uses a much larger model (Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B-Instruct), introducing a strong teacher signal that may confound the contribution attributed to MSC selection.
missing_information: No formal justification for why geometric mean is the 'correct' normalization (only empirical comparison in Appendix A.4).
How sentence boundaries are detected.
Whether sufficiency is computed under πθ before/after MFT, and which model's probabilities are used at evaluation time.
The exact form of R_format and R_cor (binary? continuous?).
Sensitivity to alpha and delta_0 hyperparameters beyond a single setting (0.5, 0.4).
ambiguous_or_unsupported: Claim that sentence-level segmentation 'avoids fragmentary truncation that may distort logical structure' is asserted without empirical comparison to token-level truncation.
Claim that percentile rank is 'robust to outliers and yields values uniformly distributed in [0,1]' — uniform distribution depends on no ties in lengths and is asserted as a generic property.

sections.name: Experiments
sections.summary: Trains SuCo on Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B/7B-Base over ~270K curated samples. Compares against base/instruct/R1-Distill and four ALRMs (AdaCoT, AdaptThink, S-GRPO, LHRMs) on 8 benchmarks across math, code, science.
key_claims: SuCo achieves the highest or near-highest accuracy at both scales.
SuCo reduces tokens by 74-76% vs R1-Distill while improving accuracy.
Ablations confirm contributions of MFT components, DCP, and sufficiency reward.
issues: Baseline fairness: SuCo is trained on a large curated corpus (270K MSC samples) refined by Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B-Instruct; AdaCoT and LHRMs are reportedly trained on the same source data but it is unclear whether they receive the same refined supervision or only raw CoTs — this could substantially impact accuracy comparisons.
AdaptThink and S-GRPO are initialized from a different base model (R1-Distill-Qwen) rather than Qwen2.5-Math-Base; this confounds the comparison.
Base model choice (Qwen2.5-Math-Base) is math-specialized; comparing to baselines on code and science benchmarks may be biased.
The 'Math-Base' row reports 22.6% on MATH500, surprisingly low for Qwen2.5-Math-Base — raises questions about evaluation protocol (e.g., chat template, decoding).
Figure 1 used Qwen3-8B (not the training base), but the experiments use Qwen2.5-Math. The motivating evidence is on a different model than the trained one.
Per-benchmark variance: only AIME25 (30 problems) and AMC23 (40 problems) are averaged over 10 runs; other small/variable benchmarks lack confidence intervals.

OOD AlpacaEval result: 'Full CoT SFT' achieves LC-WR 0.3 which is extremely low compared to DeepSeek-R1-Distill's 1.05; the meaning of these LC-WR values is unclear (they look like absolute LC-win rates, much lower than typical).

OOD evaluation 'Full CoT SFT' baseline performs dramatically worse than R1-Distill across all OOD tasks (e.g., 22.6 vs 53.3 on StrategyQA), suggesting the comparison may be unfair or the SFT baseline is degenerate.
Cross-model robustness Table 5 only tests Qwen2.5-1.5B and Llama-3.2-3B as targets and uses calibrators of similar capability — no test of whether weak calibrators (e.g., 1.5B) generate useful MSC.

Token reduction percentages are computed against R1-Distill (a particularly verbose baseline) rather than against best efficient baseline; some efficiency gains over LHRMs are modest (e.g., 7B avg: 1267 vs 1891).
missing_information: No statistical significance testing on benchmark differences.
No mention of decoding parameters (temperature, max tokens) for evaluation, especially for length comparisons.
No wall-clock or FLOPs measurement to validate efficiency claims.
No breakdown of accuracy on problems with empty MSC vs non-empty MSC.
No comparison against simpler baselines like truncation at fixed length or self-consistency early stopping.
MBPP and LiveCodeBench evaluations lack details on test execution protocol.
ambiguous_or_unsupported: Claim that SAPO 'intelligently allocates additional reasoning budget' on hard problems based on Figure 5 is plausible but only shows aggregate numbers; no analysis of within-benchmark difficulty conditioning for SAPO over MFT.

Claim that MSC is 'not merely compressed reasoning but a more effective form that filters noise' (Ablation discussion) is interpretive — there is no causal evidence that MSC removes noise rather than just shortening.

sections.name: Results
sections.summary: Reports accuracy and length gains across benchmarks, ablation results decomposing MFT and SAPO contributions, OOD generalization, calibrator robustness, and empty-CoT analysis.
key_claims: 1.5B SuCo: 53.1% avg accuracy, 7B SuCo: 72.1% — best among all baselines.
Empty CoT rate decreases with difficulty and is higher for 7B.
MSC supervision transfers across calibrator models.
issues: On MATH-500, SuCo-1.5B uses 538 tokens — markedly lower than LHRMs (1252); but on AMC23 the gap shrinks (1687 vs 2477). Some efficiency advantages are uneven and not analyzed.
Figure 6: comparing SuCo-1.5B (trained on MSC) to R1-Distill-1.5B (a different base) measures combined effects of base model and method.
Empty CoT analysis (Figure 7) shows ~30-37% empty rates which is high; whether this rate is appropriate or harmful is not validated against per-instance correctness.
Cross-model robustness (Table 5): Llama-3.2-3B target with Qwen3-14B calibrator (43.2%) underperforms Qwen3-4B calibrator (44.2%) — small inversion not explained.
missing_information: No analysis of failure cases or where SuCo underperforms (e.g., AMC23 7B: 90.3 vs S-GRPO 90.5 — SuCo is not best).
No per-difficulty breakdown of accuracy improvements (only length).
No analysis of what makes the model decide to emit empty CoT for a given problem (other than aggregated rates).
ambiguous_or_unsupported: Claim that '7B's higher empty rate reflects stronger capabilities that reduce reliance on intermediate reasoning' is plausible but not directly tested (could also reflect different reward dynamics).

sections.name: Limitations
sections.summary: Acknowledges that MSC needs ground-truth answers (open-ended limitation) and that MFT depends on distilled data and a large refinement model.
key_claims: MSC construction is restricted to verifiable-answer tasks.
Removing the 80B refinement still yields competitive results.
issues: Does not acknowledge the dependency on a strong source LRM (DeepSeek-R1 distilled data) for full CoT generation.
Does not discuss potential bias from problem-adaptive threshold using length as difficulty proxy.
No discussion of robustness to noisy answers or partially correct answers.
missing_information: No discussion of compute cost of MSC construction (running sentence-level scoring of 270K trajectories).
No discussion of why scaling to >7B or other base families was not attempted.
ambiguous_or_unsupported: Claim that 'once trained, the model internalizes adaptive reasoning as a general capability' is plausible but not directly tested in adversarial OOD settings.

sections.name: Conclusion
sections.summary: Recaps MSC and SuCo as a continuous adaptive reasoning solution achieving better accuracy and efficiency. Points to open-ended and agentic future work.
key_claims: Models can perform better with less reasoning.
SuCo learns to calibrate effort without external controllers.
supporting_evidence: Refers back to main experiments and ablations.
issues: Generalization claim ('better with less reasoning') is supported only on verifiable-answer benchmarks where MSC can be defined.
missing_information: No reflection on when the approach may fail (e.g., problems where ground-truth answer is short and trivially predictable from prompt).

sections.name: Supplementary
sections.summary: Provides ablations on L_min, EMA rate, tolerance epsilon, sufficiency metric (geometric vs joint vs arithmetic mean), cross- vs intra-domain percentile, dataset statistics, sources, preprocessing, MSC construction, refinement prompt, and refinement examples.
key_claims: L_min=5, EMA=0.1, epsilon=2 are optimal.
Geometric mean outperforms joint and arithmetic mean sufficiency.
Cross-domain percentile is robust to domain-specific length distributions.
MSC refinement bridges logical gaps without distorting reasoning.
issues: Appendix A.4 (sufficiency metric ablation) shows Full CoT SFT at 38.4% but Joint Probability variant at 40.1% — only marginally above. The relative gains of geometric mean (45.7%) could partially be due to length distribution effects from threshold satisfaction patterns, not just metric properties.
Filtering criterion (5) 'non-English content' is mentioned but no analysis of what was removed.
LLM-based quality assessment for MSC filtering risks circularity since the same family of models is used for both refinement and judging.
MinHash deduplication parameters not specified.
Refinement examples (Figs 10-11) show the refined version sometimes adds substantial new reasoning (Fig 10) — this contradicts the claim that refinement does not modify reasoning content.
missing_information: No information on training compute budget for MFT and SAPO stages.
No ablation on alpha and delta_0 jointly; only single setting (0.5, 0.4) is justified post-hoc.
No analysis of MSC quality variation across source datasets.
No reporting of refinement model's failure modes or quality of refined samples.
ambiguous_or_unsupported: Claim in Appendix A.4 that geometric mean is 'equally sensitive to all answer tokens' is mathematically true but the link from that property to better MSC selection is asserted, not demonstrated.

Claim that intra-domain vs cross-domain percentile yields 'nearly identical performance' could be because the dataset mix already makes both equivalent in practice; not tested under heavily imbalanced mixes.

claims.claim: MSC (Minimal Sufficient CoT) achieves higher accuracy than full CoT with substantially fewer tokens across all MATH difficulty levels.
claims.evidence_in_paper: Figure 1 shows MSC outperforms full CoT in accuracy at every difficulty level L1–L5 on Qwen3-8B with substantially fewer reasoning tokens.
claims.notes: Figure 1 is computed on Qwen3-8B (not the model used in main experiments) and uses oracle access to ground truth y* to construct MSC — this is not a fair test-time comparison; it shows MSC exists, not that a model can generate it without oracle info.

claims.claim: Geometric-mean sufficiency formulation is superior to joint probability and arithmetic mean baselines.
claims.evidence_in_paper: Appendix Table 8: geometric mean 45.7% / 1,344 tokens vs joint 40.1% / 2,573 and arithmetic 43.1% / 1,578.
claims.notes: Single ablation on Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B; the relative gap is clear, justification of choice is empirical rather than theoretical.

claims.claim: Problem-adaptive thresholds outperform any single static threshold.
claims.evidence_in_paper: Table 2 Threshold rows: MFT (adaptive) 45.7%/1,344 tokens vs best static δ=0.7 at 44.0%/1,346.
claims.notes: Improvement over best static is modest (~1.7 pts) but consistent at comparable token budget.

claims.claim: SuCo outperforms full-CoT and adaptive baselines (AdaCoT, AdaptThink, S-GRPO, LHRMs) on both accuracy and token usage across 8 benchmarks at 1.5B and 7B scales.
claims.evidence_in_paper: Table 1: SuCo-1.5B avg 53.1%/1,483 vs LHRMs 50.5%/2,055 and R1-Distill 45.2%/5,736; SuCo-7B avg 72.1%/1,267 vs LHRMs 68.6%/1,891 and R1-Distill 63.2%/5,239.
claims.notes: Comparison fairness is partly addressed (AdaCoT/LHRMs re-trained on same data), but AdaptThink/S-GRPO use a different base (DeepSeek-R1-Distill) — confounds attribution. No statistical significance reported; small benchmarks (AMC23 n=40, AIME25 n=30) averaged over 10 runs but no variance/std.

claims.claim: Both Dynamic Complexity Pool (DCP) and Sufficiency-Aware Reward (R_suff) contribute to SAPO performance.
claims.evidence_in_paper: Table 3: full SAPO 53.1%/1,483 vs w/o DCP 52.9%/1,642 and w/o R_suff 52.7%/2,053.
claims.notes: Accuracy differences are very small (~0.2–0.4 pts) and likely within noise; the components mainly affect token efficiency rather than accuracy. No significance test.

claims.claim: SAPO performs adaptive allocation (less reasoning on easy tasks, more on hard) rather than uniform compression.
claims.evidence_in_paper: Figure 5: MFT→SAPO reduces tokens on GSM8K (382→304) and MATH500 (884→538) but increases on AMC23/AIME25/Live-V6 with corresponding accuracy gains.
claims.notes: Per-benchmark trade-off pattern is consistent with claim.

claims.claim: SuCo generalizes to out-of-domain tasks (StrategyQA, CSQA, AlpacaEval).
claims.notes: Only three OOD tasks; Full CoT SFT baseline performs surprisingly poorly (22.6%/19.4%/0.3 LC-WR) — suggesting catastrophic forgetting from math/code SFT rather than that SuCo specifically transfers reasoning well. AlpacaEval LC-WR of 2.4 is very low in absolute terms.

claims.claim: MSC construction is robust across calibrator models (Qwen3-4B/14B, DS-R1-Distill-7B).
claims.evidence_in_paper: Table 5: all three calibrators yield ~44% accuracy on Qwen2.5-1.5B/Llama-3.2-3B vs Full CoT SFT 37–38%.
claims.notes: Reasonable evidence of robustness within the tested calibrator family; all are Qwen/DeepSeek-derived models.

claims.claim: SuCo exhibits stronger difficulty-conditioned scaling than base LRM (5.5× vs 3.1× L5/L1 token ratio).
claims.evidence_in_paper: Figure 6 and text: SuCo L1 mean 302, L5 mean 1,665; DS-R1-Distill L1 2,745, L5 8,465.
claims.notes: Numbers in figure support the claim; interpretation as 'genuine difficulty-conditioned allocation' is reasonable though not directly verified.

claims.claim: MFT alone surpasses Full CoT SFT with ~26% of the tokens.
claims.notes: Note that 'Full CoT SFT' starts from Qwen2.5-Math-Base which is small; R1-Distill (a different distillation pipeline) achieves 45.2%, comparable to MFT. So MFT's strong improvement over Full CoT SFT may partly reflect base-model SFT inefficiency, not just MSC superiority.

claims.claim: MSC provides a principled sufficiency criterion enabling continuous rather than discrete reasoning control.
claims.evidence_in_paper: Section 3.2 formalization with adaptive threshold δ(x) = δ0 + α·C(x); contrast with related work (Section 2).
claims.notes: Conceptual framing is novel relative to discrete-mode baselines, but 'continuous' control is somewhat overstated: at inference, the model still produces a single trajectory; continuity is in the training threshold, not in user-controllable behavior.

claims.claim: Reasoning length is a valid proxy for problem complexity.
claims.evidence_in_paper: Section 3.2 cites Figure 1 ('reasoning length serves as a practical proxy for problem complexity, as empirically supported in Figure 1').
claims.notes: Figure 1 only shows average length increases with MATH difficulty level; this is a correlational, not causal, justification. The proxy may conflate verbosity with hardness — e.g., easy problems with formal derivations may be long.

claims.claim: The percentile-based complexity estimation is robust to outliers and outperforms Min-Max and Log-Scaled alternatives.
claims.evidence_in_paper: Table 2 Complexity rows: percentile (via MFT) 45.7%/1,344 vs Min-Max 38.6%/1,160, Log-Scaled 44.5%/1,399.

claims.claim: MSC refinement improves coherence and reduces length.
claims.evidence_in_paper: Table 2 Refinement: w/o refine 43.5%/2,189 vs MFT 45.7%/1,344.
claims.notes: Improvement is real but conflates refinement with use of Qwen3-Next-80B teacher distillation — author acknowledges this in limitations. The refined MSC may inherit knowledge from a much larger model.

claims.claim: SuCo enables 'autonomous' reasoning control without external intervention.
claims.evidence_in_paper: Stated in introduction and contributions; SAPO trains the policy to internalize sufficiency.
claims.notes: Training process uses oracle y* probabilities to define MSC; the deployed model is autonomous, but the framing implies a stronger novelty than the prior work LHRMs/AdaCoT, which also do not require runtime intervention.

unsupported_claims: Models 'can achieve stronger performance with less reasoning' as a general principle — Figure 1 evidence uses oracle ground truth to select MSC prefixes, which is not what a deployed model does; the actual deployed SuCo improves accuracy but the causal mechanism (less reasoning → higher accuracy) is not isolated from confounders like training data refinement by an 80B teacher.

Claim of 'continuous' reasoning spectrum control — the methodology produces a single policy and a single trajectory at inference; the continuity is in training-time threshold construction, not in a controllable inference-time knob.
Implicit claim that SuCo's gains are due to the sufficiency formulation rather than the additional teacher-refined training signal from Qwen3-Next-80B.
overstated_claims: 'SuCo consistently achieves improvements in both accuracy and reasoning efficiency' — true on average but on some benchmarks (e.g., AMC23 7B: 90.3 vs S-GRPO 90.5) accuracy is slightly lower; presented as uniformly best.

'Models can perform better with less reasoning' (Section 1 framing) — what is shown is that careful selection/refinement of a short CoT can outperform a verbose one, not that less reasoning per se is better.
'Without intervention' / 'autonomous' framing relative to LHRMs/AdaCoT — the distinction is more incremental than the abstract suggests.
Strong OOD generalization claim from only 3 tasks, with very low AlpacaEval LC-WR (2.4) in absolute terms.
claims_requiring_external_verification: Baseline numbers for AdaCoT, AdaptThink, S-GRPO, LHRMs at matched model scales — these are re-implementations with mixed base models (some re-trained on SuCo's data, some not), and reported numbers should be checked against original papers.
DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-1.5B/7B reported accuracies on AIME25, MATH500, GPQA-D (Table 1) compared to official numbers.
Whether the geometric-mean sufficiency metric meaningfully differs from token-level perplexity-based confidence measures used in prior calibration literature (novelty positioning).
Whether the 'dynamic complexity pool' with EMA materially differs from prior length-based curriculum/reward shaping methods in RLHF literature.
Validity of using percentile rank of length as a complexity proxy — particularly whether it correlates with true problem difficulty independent of model verbosity.

novelty_axes.claimed_contribution: Formalizing 'Minimal Sufficient CoT (MSC)' as the shortest reasoning prefix that suffices for a correct answer, and framing adaptive reasoning as continuous (vs. discrete-mode) calibration.
novelty_axes.why_it_matters: The paper's headline conceptual contribution. If prior work already defined a 'sufficient prefix' criterion or already cast adaptive reasoning along a continuous spectrum, the framing is largely repackaging.
search_keywords: minimal sufficient chain of thought prefix
novelty_axes.search_question: Has prior work formalized a 'minimal sufficient' CoT prefix or used confidence-of-ground-truth-given-prefix as a stopping/sufficiency criterion?
novelty_axes.closest_prior_work_ref: DEER (Yang et al. 2025) [similar_prior_work #1]; Chain-of-Draft (Xu et al. 2025) [similar_prior_work #5]

novelty_axes.claimed_contribution: (a) Geometric-mean of per-token ground-truth probabilities as a sufficiency signal; (b) problem-adaptive threshold δ(x)=δ0+α·C(x) with percentile-based length complexity; (c) two-stage MFT + SAPO with (d) EMA-updated dynamic complexity pool and (e) sufficiency-aware reward penalizing over- and under-thinking.
novelty_axes.why_it_matters: Most of the paper's machinery; novelty depends on whether existing efficient-reasoning methods already use (i) per-token confidence-based truncation, (ii) length/budget-conditioned reward shaping, and (iii) online complexity tracking.

novelty_axes.closest_prior_work_ref: DEER [similar_prior_work #1]; S-GRPO [similar_prior_work #2]; ThinkPrune [similar_prior_work #3]; L1 [similar_prior_work #4]; Kimi K1.5 length penalty [similar_prior_work #6]; O1-Pruner [similar_prior_work #7]; DAST [similar_prior_work #8]

novelty_axes.why_it_matters: SFT-then-RL is the standard reasoning-model recipe; the contribution is the specific data construction and reward design.

MSC data construction CoT truncation refinement
novelty_axes.search_question: Is the SFT-on-short-CoT followed by length-aware GRPO recipe already standard?

novelty_axes.claimed_contribution: Comprehensive demonstration that MSC training yields ~75% token reduction with consistent accuracy gains across math/code/science at 1.5B/7B, plus OOD transfer and cross-calibrator robustness.
novelty_axes.why_it_matters: If the empirical gap over close baselines (LHRMs, S-GRPO) is large and reproducible, the empirical contribution stands even if the method is composed of known parts.

AdaCoT AdaptThink S-GRPO LHRMs benchmark comparison
